context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on the city case study data table rn. idk how to find or organize stuff like GDP, income per capita, and the Köppen climate zone for my city.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — those are key pieces of the table! Before I point you anywhere, what have you already tried? Have you looked up any of these data points yet, and if so, where did you search?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad question about where to find data and how to organize it. Rather than listing sources for them, I should ask a targeted question to understand what they've already tried, then guide them to think through reliable sources on their own. This keeps the discovery process student-led and avoids doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: kinda just googled the city name + “GDP” and got a bunch of diff numbers, which confused me tbh. I haven’t really found the Köppen thing yet either.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really common issue — GDP figures vary depending on the source, the year, and whether they're measuring the city, metro area, or region. So before anything else: what do you think would make one source more reliable than another for this kind of data?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine attempt but is confused by conflicting data from general searches. I should validate that this is a common issue, then guide them to think critically about source reliability rather than just giving them a list of databases. For Köppen, I'll prompt them to think about what kind of source would have that information. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe govt sites or big orgs like World Bank? but I’m not sure how to tell if it’s city-only vs metro, and that’s where I get lost.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on those sources. Now think about it this way — for your assignment, which would be more meaningful to report: city-only or metro area data? And why might that choice matter for understanding your city's climate vulnerabilities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with identifying reliable sources. I should affirm this and then push them to think about why the city vs. metro distinction matters for their assignment, rather than resolving it for them. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I think city-only makes more sense since the assignment is about the city itself, but ngl metro might be easier to find. maybe it matters bc the population/income could look way bigger if it includes suburbs?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — suburbs can skew figures significantly. So here's a practical question: if you do end up using metro data because it's more available, what could you do in your table to make that transparent to the reader? And once you've thought about that, want to take a crack at the Köppen zone?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well here — they've identified a real methodological tension and understood why the distinction matters. I should affirm their thinking and push them to decide how to handle this in their table, rather than resolving it for them. I can also nudge them toward the Köppen question since they haven't tackled that yet.

exchanges.student: I could label it clearly like “metro GDP” and maybe note the year/source so it’s not misleading. For Köppen, is that basically just like tropical/dry/temperate or am I oversimplifying it?
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking on the labeling — that's exactly the kind of transparency that makes data useful. On Köppen, you're not wrong that those are rough categories, but the system is more specific than that. What city are you working with? Have you tried looking up its specific Köppen classification yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a good solution for data transparency. I should affirm this briefly. On Köppen, they have the basic idea right but are underselling it — I should prompt them to look into it more specifically for their city rather than explaining the system to them.

exchanges.student: I’m doing Jakarta. I haven’t found a solid source yet, just random websites saying tropical rainforest maybe?
exchanges.tutor: Tropical rainforest is a reasonable starting point for Jakarta — but what kind of source would you trust to confirm a climate classification? Think about who actually produces or uses that kind of scientific data.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a tentative answer for Jakarta's Köppen zone but hasn't verified it with a reliable source. I should prompt them to think about what kind of source would be authoritative for climate classification data, rather than confirming or denying the tropical rainforest answer.

exchanges.student: maybe a climate map from a university or like a meteorology agency? if I find “Af” from one of those, that should count right?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right kind of source to look for. Go find one and verify it — once you have it, make sure you record the source in your table just like you planned to do with the GDP data. How are you feeling about filling in the rest of the table at this point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well about source reliability and has identified a specific Köppen code (Af). I should affirm their source instinct and nudge them to actually go find and verify it rather than having me confirm it. They're close to being able to complete this section on their own.

exchanges.student: better tbh, I think I was overthinking it. I still need help figuring out where to get race/ethnicity data for Jakarta tho, bc idk if that’s even reported the same way there.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — race/ethnicity categories are not universal and differ country to country. So who do you think would be the primary authority on demographic data for an Indonesian city like Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has raised a genuinely interesting methodological question about cross-national comparability of demographic data. Rather than directing them to a source, I should prompt them to think about who collects demographic data in Indonesia and what categories might be used locally.

exchanges.student: probably Indonesia’s stats office/census, right? I’m just not sure if I should use ethnicity, religion, or something else if the categories don’t match the assignment exactly.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the source. And think back to how you handled the city vs. metro issue — what approach did you use there that might apply here too?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the right source and is now wrestling with a genuine methodological question about category mismatch. This is good critical thinking. I should affirm their source choice and push them to think about how to handle the mismatch in their table rather than resolving it for them. This mirrors the same transparency solution they already came up with for GDP.

exchanges.student: I guess same idea: use the closest official category they actually report, then label it clearly and note it’s not a perfect match. does that sound okay for the table?
exchanges.tutor: That's solid reasoning and good methodological consistency. You've worked through the main challenges here — I think you have what you need to start filling in the table. Give it a go on your own, and reach out if you hit a specific snag!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently transferred their earlier reasoning to a new problem — this is exactly the kind of learning we want to see. I should affirm this briefly and encourage them to move forward and attempt the table on their own now, since their core confusions seem resolved.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student with questions rather than giving direct answers. The learning rationale (active reasoning, skill development) is never articulated to the student.

At the end of the conversation the tutor says 'you've worked through the main challenges here' and sends the student off, but does not prompt any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the methodological approach (label clearly, note source, use closest official category) worked as a general strategy. No transfer or reflection moment is created.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic practice throughout the conversation, consistently asking guiding questions rather than providing direct answers, building on student responses, and maintaining good assignment anchoring. The student was led to discover key methodological principles (source reliability, transparency in labeling, adapting categories) through their own reasoning. Minor deductions apply under 1.3: the tutor never explained its pedagogical rationale to the student (why it was asking questions rather than just answering), and the closing turn missed an opportunity to prompt genuine reflection on what the student learned and how the approach could transfer to future research tasks. No other rubric criteria were triggered.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic practice throughout the conversation, consistently asking guiding questions rather than providing direct answers, building on student responses, and maintaining good assignment anchoring. The student was led to discover key methodological principles (source reliability, transparency in labeling, adapting categories) through their own reasoning. Minor deductions apply under 1.3: the tutor never explained its pedagogical rationale to the student (why it was asking questions rather than just answering), and the closing turn missed an opportunity to prompt genuine reflection on what the student learned and how the approach could transfer to future research tasks. No other rubric criteria were triggered.
